text: {"overallConclusion":"The source overgeneralizes the findings of the trial, claiming universal permanent fall prevention despite limited evidence.","inferences":[{"type":"evidence-for","premiseIndexes":[0],"conclusionIndex":1,"warrant":"Empirical results from a controlled trial can be used as evidence to support claims about a program's effectiveness.","strength":"weak","defeaters":["The trial involved only 42 participants","The age range was limited to 65‑75","The follow‑up period was only eight weeks"]},{"type":"undercuts","premiseIndexes":[0],"conclusionIndex":1,"warrant":"When evidence is limited in scope (sample size, age range, duration), it weakens broader claims that extend beyond that scope.","strength":"moderate","defeaters":["If additional data showed similar effects in broader populations and longer periods","If the claim is qualified to reflect the limited scope"]}]}
